context: Current closing price of Duke Energy (DUK): $130.40 USD as of 2026-04-13.
justification_text:
My point estimates reflect a mild upward drift from what I'd have taken as the prevailing price level at the time of forecasting. At the one-day horizon, the estimate of around 130.6 is essentially an anchoring to recent price action with very little expected directional movement, since intraday noise tends to dominate over any fundamental signal on such a short timeframe. For the one-week horizon, I nudged the estimate slightly higher to 131.0, reflecting a modest positive drift assumption consistent with a low-volatility, slightly bullish baseline. At one month, the 132.0 estimate embeds a somewhat larger drift, consistent with long-run equity or asset appreciation tendencies, though still conservative in magnitude. Across all three horizons, the point estimates largely assume continuation of recent trend rather than any sharp reversal.

The confidence interval widths widen substantially as the horizon extends, which reflects the square-root-of-time scaling that characterizes random walk uncertainty. At one day, the 90% interval of roughly plus or minus 3 points is tight because there is simply less time for price to wander. By one week, the interval expands to a range of about 12 points, and by one month it reaches roughly 24 points, capturing the compounding uncertainty over more trading sessions. Implied or historical volatility of the underlying asset was the primary input shaping these widths, alongside liquidity conditions and the general macro environment at the time.

Specific risks that could push outcomes outside these intervals include sudden macroeconomic data surprises such as inflation prints, employment reports, or central bank decisions that shift rate expectations sharply. Geopolitical shocks, unexpected earnings announcements from heavily weighted constituents, or a broad risk-off episode triggered by credit stress could all produce moves well beyond the stated bands. On the upside, a stronger-than-expected policy pivot or a resolution of trade or geopolitical tensions could drive prices above the upper bound, particularly over the one-month horizon where there is more time for such catalysts to materialize and compound.

It is also worth noting that my intervals are calibrated under relatively normal market conditions. In periods of elevated realized volatility or market dislocation, fat-tailed distributions mean that even a 90% confidence interval based on historical volatility assumptions will be violated more frequently than the stated 10% tail probability would suggest. Black swan events, by definition, are not well captured by standard statistical frameworks, so these stated intervals should be understood as a reasonable central tendency tool rather than a guarantee of containment.
